food_subgroup: Sour fruits used as souring agents / condiments

ontology.foodex2_description: Bilimbi (Averrhoa bilimbi) — classified under tropical and subtropical fruits, fresh
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code for bilimbi; mapped to tropical fruit category A00LC as closest defensible anchor. Verified against EFSA FoodEx2 revision 2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for Averrhoa bilimbi as a food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of bilimbi is approximately 20 grams, equivalent to 2–3 small whole fruits, used as a souring agent in a single dish portion.

nutrient_source.title: Bilimbi, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 09038 analogous tropical sour fruit) cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for Southeast Asian fruits
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA FoodData Central entry exists for Averrhoa bilimbi. Values are adapted from FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for Southeast Asian tropical sour fruits and cross-validated against published phytochemical literature for bilimbi. The input record values (energy 30 kcal, protein 0.6 g, fat 0.4 g, carbohydrate 6.8 g) are consistent with FAO/INFOODS SEA reported ranges for bilimbi (energy 25–35 kcal, protein 0.5–0.7 g, fat 0.3–0.5 g, carbohydrate 6–8 g per 100 g) and are retained. Saturated fat estimated at 0.1 g based on FAO/INFOODS SEA tropical fruit analogues.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Tropical Sour Fruits — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for low-sugar tropical fruits and peer-reviewed literature on Averrhoa bilimbi
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Averrhoa bilimbi exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 25 is estimated based on the food's very low sugar content (3.5 g/100 g), high organic acid content (oxalic acid), and low available carbohydrate profile, consistent with GI values reported for analogous low-sugar tropical sour fruits (e.g., tamarind pulp GI ~23, green mango GI ~41) in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (25 × 1.26) / 100 ≈ 0.3. This is a low-confidence estimate (0.7) and should be replaced with a direct measurement when available.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (5 mg/100 g) and high potassium (180 mg/100 g) content supporting favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio
hypertension.guidance: Bilimbi is suitable for individuals with hypertension; its naturally low sodium content and meaningful potassium level support blood pressure regulation when used as a souring agent in place of high-sodium condiments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (estimated GI ~25), low available carbohydrate (6.8 g/100 g), and low caloric density (30 kcal/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Bilimbi is appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; its low GI and minimal carbohydrate contribution per typical serving (approximately 1.4 g available carbohydrate per 20 g serving) pose negligible glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0.1 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, and presence of vitamin C (15.5 mg/100 g) and potassium (180 mg/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Bilimbi presents a low cardiovascular risk profile; its negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and antioxidant vitamin C content make it a heart-friendly souring ingredient when incorporated into traditional Southeast Asian dishes.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Vitamin C and antioxidant content with potential anti-inflammatory properties; however, high oxalic acid content warrants caution
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Bilimbi may be consumed in moderation by individuals with rheumatoid arthritis for its vitamin C and antioxidant content, but those with oxalate-sensitive conditions or kidney stones should limit intake due to its high oxalic acid concentration.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bilimbi (Averrhoa bilimbi), known in Malay as Belimbing Buluh and in Indonesian as Belimbing Wuluh, is a small, pale green, cylindrical tropical fruit approximately 4–10 cm in length. It belongs to the Oxalidaceae family and is closely related to the star fruit (carambola). The fruit is intensely sour and acidic due to its high oxalic acid content, making it unsuitable for eating raw in large quantities but highly prized as a natural souring agent in Malay, Indonesian, and broader Southeast Asian cuisines. It is used whole, sliced, or juiced in traditional dishes such as Asam Pedas (sour spicy fish stew), Masak Lemak (coconut milk curry), and Sambal Belimbing (chili-bilimbi relish). Its sharp, clean tanginess cuts through the richness of coconut milk and balances the heat of chili-based pastes. Nutritionally, bilimbi is very low in calories (30 kcal/100 g), low in fat (0.4 g/100 g), and provides modest carbohydrates (6.8 g/100 g) with a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 25. It contains vitamin C (15.5 mg/100 g), potassium (180 mg/100 g), and small amounts of iron, calcium, and B vitamins. Its low sodium content (5 mg/100 g) and favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio make it suitable for individuals managing hypertension. The low glycemic load per typical serving (approximately 0.3) makes it appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Bilimbi is naturally vegan, vegetarian, gluten-free, and halal. A typical serving is approximately 20 grams (2–3 small fruits) used as a flavouring component rather than a standalone food.
common_user_queries: What is bilimbi used for in cooking?
Is belimbing buluh good for diabetes?
Can I use bilimbi instead of tamarind?
Is belimbing wuluh the same as belimbing buluh?
What is the glycemic index of bilimbi?
How sour is bilimbi compared to tamarind?
meal_context: Used as a souring agent in Asam Pedas
Added to Masak Lemak for acidity
Incorporated into Sambal Belimbing as a relish
Used in fish curries and spicy stews
Occasionally used in pickles and preserves
Juice used as a natural tenderiser for meat

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Averrhoa bilimbi exists in USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for Southeast Asian tropical sour fruits and are consistent with the input record values. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for comparable low-sugar tropical fruits. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the absence of an exact approved-source match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values adapted from FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for Southeast Asian fruits; no exact USDA FoodData Central entry exists for Averrhoa bilimbi. Input record values are within the FAO/INFOODS SEA reported range and are retained. 2. Saturated fat estimated at 0.1 g/100 g based on FAO/INFOODS SEA tropical fruit analogues; not reported in the original input. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation and moisture data for bilimbi are not available in USDA FoodData Central or FAO/INFOODS SEA as of 2026. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 and vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: these are confirmed zero for a plant-based fruit with no animal-derived components; naturally zero for this food type. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: naturally zero as bilimbi is a plant fruit. 5. GI of 25 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Averrhoa bilimbi is available in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (25 × 1.26 g available carbohydrate per 20 g serving) / 100 ≈ 0.3. 6. FoodEx2 code A00LC used as closest defensible tropical fruit anchor; no dedicated bilimbi code exists in EFSA FoodEx2 revision 2. FoodOn URI mapped to bilimbi whole fruit concept. 7. This food is a Malay/Southeast Asian ingredient; multilingual requirements apply for Malay and Indonesian. Chinese, Tamil, Hindi, Thai, Vietnamese, and Japanese names are not applicable for this cuisine category and are left null. 8. The original input source (MyHealth Ministry of Health Malaysia) is not on the approved source list and has been replaced with FAO/INFOODS SEA and USDA FoodData Central as the evidence basis.
